metadata.title: Aman Agarwal — Professional Summary
metadata.text: Aman Agarwal — Senior Software Engineer. Senior Software Engineer with 7+ years of experience building scalable web applications and AI-powered systems. Passionate about leveraging LLMs and RAG architectures to create intelligent user experiences. Experienced in TypeScript, Python, and cloud-native development.

metadata.title: Senior Software Engineer at TechCorp AI
metadata.text:
Senior Software Engineer at TechCorp AI (2023-01 to present), San Francisco, CA.
Leading the development of AI-powered search and retrieval systems. Designed and implemented a RAG pipeline that serves 500k+ queries daily with sub-200ms P95 latency. Built a citation grounding system that reduced hallucination rates by 60%. Architected a multi-tenant vector store using Pinecone with namespace isolation. Mentoring a team of 4 engineers on LLM integration best practices.

metadata.text:
ntoring a team of 4 engineers on LLM integration best practices.
Key skills: TypeScript, Python, LangChain, Pinecone, Next.js, AWS.

Implemented real-time collaboration features using WebSockets. Led migration from JavaScript to TypeScript across the entire codebase (200k+ lines). Introduced CI/CD pipelines with GitHub Actions reducing deployment time by 80%.

Software Engineer at BigBank Financial (2019-06 to 2021-02), New York, NY.
Developed real-time fraud detection microservices processing 10M+ transactions daily using Kafka and Python. Built internal dashboards with React that reduced fraud investigation time by 45%. Implemented event-driven architecture patterns for transaction monitoring. Collaborated with data science team to deploy ML models into production serving infrastructure.
Key skills: Python, Kafka, React, Kubernetes, PostgreSQL, Redis.

Built responsive websites and web applications for 30+ clients using React and WordPress. Introduced automated testing practices that caught 40% more bugs before deployment. Set up CI/CD pipelines with Jenkins that cut deployment time by 70%. Developed custom WordPress plugins and themes for enterprise clients.

Focus on distributed systems and machine learning. Senior thesis on 'Efficient Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search in High-Dimensional Spaces' — directly relevant to modern vector databases. Dean's List 2015-2017.

metadata.title: Building Production RAG Systems: Lessons from the Trenches
metadata.text:
From blog post: "Building Production RAG Systems: Lessons from the Trenches"
After spending the past year building retrieval-augmented generation systems at TechCorp AI, I want to share the practical lessons that no tutorial covers.

metadata.section: The Chunking Problem Nobody Talks About (part 1/3)
metadata.title: Building Production RAG Systems: Lessons from the Trenches
metadata.text:
From blog post: "Building Production RAG Systems: Lessons from the Trenches"
Section: The Chunking Problem Nobody Talks About
Everyone focuses on the LLM, but retrieval quality is 80% of the battle. The single biggest lever you have is your chunking strategy.

metadata.section: The Chunking Problem Nobody Talks About (part 2/3)
metadata.title: Building Production RAG Systems: Lessons from the Trenches
metadata.text:
le. The single biggest lever you have is your chunking strategy.

We started with naive fixed-size chunking (split every 500 characters) and our relevance scores were terrible. Switching to structure-aware chunking — where we respect document boundaries like headings, paragraphs, and code blocks — improved retrieval precision by 35%.

metadata.section: The Chunking Problem Nobody Talks About (part 3/3)
metadata.title: Building Production RAG Systems: Lessons from the Trenches
metadata.text:
ragraphs, and code blocks — improved retrieval precision by 35%.

The key insight: your chunks should be **semantically self-contained**. A chunk that starts mid-paragraph and ends mid-sentence is useless because its embedding is a noisy average of two unrelated topics.

metadata.section: Hybrid Search is Worth the Complexity (part 1/2)
metadata.title: Building Production RAG Systems: Lessons from the Trenches
metadata.text:
From blog post: "Building Production RAG Systems: Lessons from the Trenches"
Section: Hybrid Search is Worth the Complexity
Pure vector search has a blind spot: exact keyword matches. If a user searches for "KPFX-2847" (a specific error code), vector similarity might not surface the right document because embedding models aren't great with identifiers.

metadata.section: Hybrid Search is Worth the Complexity (part 2/2)
metadata.title: Building Production RAG Systems: Lessons from the Trenches
metadata.text:
document because embedding models aren't great with identifiers.

We solved this with hybrid search: combine vector similarity (for semantic understanding) with BM25 (for exact keyword matching), then re-rank the merged results. This added complexity but improved our end-to-end accuracy from 78% to 92%.

metadata.title: Building Production RAG Systems: Lessons from the Trenches
metadata.text:
From blog post: "Building Production RAG Systems: Lessons from the Trenches"
Section: Citation Grounding Eliminates Hallucination
The most impactful change we made was forcing the LLM to cite its sources. By numbering context chunks and instructing the model to reference them explicitly, we could verify every claim. If the LLM generates a statement without a citation, we flag it for review.

This simple pattern reduced hallucination in our production system by 60%.

metadata.section: Embedding Model Selection Matters More Than LLM Choice
metadata.title: Building Production RAG Systems: Lessons from the Trenches
metadata.text:
From blog post: "Building Production RAG Systems: Lessons from the Trenches"
Section: Embedding Model Selection Matters More Than LLM Choice
Counterintuitive finding: upgrading our embedding model from `text-embedding-ada-002` to `text-embedding-3-large` improved answer quality more than switching from GPT-3.5 to GPT-4 for generation. Better embeddings mean better retrieval, which means the LLM gets better context to work with.

metadata.title: Building Production RAG Systems: Lessons from the Trenches
metadata.text:
From blog post: "Building Production RAG Systems: Lessons from the Trenches"
Section: Conclusion
RAG is not just "throw documents into a vector DB and query." It's a system design problem where chunking, retrieval strategy, and prompt engineering all interact. Get the fundamentals right before reaching for more complex solutions.

metadata.title: Migrating 200k Lines of JavaScript to TypeScript: A Practical Guide
metadata.text:
From blog post: "Migrating 200k Lines of JavaScript to TypeScript: A Practical Guide"
At StartupXYZ, we had a growing JavaScript codebase that was becoming increasingly difficult to maintain. Runtime type errors were our #1 source of production bugs. Here's how we migrated to TypeScript without stopping feature work.

metadata.section: The Strategy: Gradual, Not Big Bang (part 1/3)
metadata.title: Migrating 200k Lines of JavaScript to TypeScript: A Practical Guide
metadata.text:
From blog post: "Migrating 200k Lines of JavaScript to TypeScript: A Practical Guide"
Section: The Strategy: Gradual, Not Big Bang
We rejected the "rewrite everything" approach. Instead, we adopted a gradual migration:

metadata.section: The Strategy: Gradual, Not Big Bang (part 2/3)
metadata.title: Migrating 200k Lines of JavaScript to TypeScript: A Practical Guide
metadata.text:
e everything" approach. Instead, we adopted a gradual migration:

1. **Configure TypeScript in "loose" mode** — set `strict: false`, `allowJs: true`, and `noImplicitAny: false`. This lets `.ts` and `.js` files coexist.
2. **Rename files one module at a time** — starting with leaf modules (utilities, helpers) that have no dependencies.
3. **Add types incrementally** — each PR that touches a file must add types to the functions it modifies.

metadata.section: The Strategy: Gradual, Not Big Bang (part 3/3)
metadata.title: Migrating 200k Lines of JavaScript to TypeScript: A Practical Guide
metadata.text:
that touches a file must add types to the functions it modifies.
4. **Tighten the config over time** — once a module is fully typed, enable `strict` for that directory.

metadata.title: Migrating 200k Lines of JavaScript to TypeScript: A Practical Guide
metadata.text:
From blog post: "Migrating 200k Lines of JavaScript to TypeScript: A Practical Guide"
Section: The Results
After 6 months of gradual migration:

- 95% of files converted to TypeScript
- Runtime type errors dropped by 73%
- Developer onboarding time decreased by 40% (types serve as documentation)
- IDE autocomplete and refactoring became dramatically more reliable

metadata.title: Migrating 200k Lines of JavaScript to TypeScript: A Practical Guide
metadata.text:
From blog post: "Migrating 200k Lines of JavaScript to TypeScript: A Practical Guide"
Section: Key Lessons
**Start with your data models.** Defining interfaces for your API responses and database models gives you the biggest bang for your buck. Once your data shapes are typed, type errors propagate naturally through the codebase.

**Don't use `any` as an escape hatch.** It's tempting, but each `any` is a hole in your type safety net. Use `unknown` instead and narrow with type guards.

metadata.title: Migrating 200k Lines of JavaScript to TypeScript: A Practical Guide
metadata.text:
e safety net. Use `unknown` instead and narrow with type guards.

**Invest in shared type packages.** We created a `@company/types` package shared between frontend and backend. This eliminated an entire class of API contract bugs.

metadata.title: Migrating 200k Lines of JavaScript to TypeScript: A Practical Guide
metadata.text:
From blog post: "Migrating 200k Lines of JavaScript to TypeScript: A Practical Guide"
Section: Conclusion
TypeScript migration doesn't have to be all-or-nothing. The gradual approach let us maintain velocity while systematically improving code quality. The investment paid for itself within 3 months through reduced bug rates alone.

metadata.title: AI Portfolio Chat
metadata.text:
Project: AI Portfolio Chat (2025, in-progress).
A RAG-powered conversational portfolio website that answers questions about my career using retrieval-augmented generation. Features streaming responses, source citations, and a PII safety layer. Built with Next.js App Router, Vercel AI SDK, Pinecone, and Tailwind CSS.
Built with: Next.js, TypeScript, Vercel AI SDK, Pinecone, Tailwind CSS.
Live at: https://amanagarwal.dev
Source code: https://github.com/aman/ai-portfolio

metadata.title: Enterprise RAG Search
metadata.text:
Project: Enterprise RAG Search (2024, production).
Internal knowledge base search engine at TechCorp AI. Indexes 500k+ documents across Confluence, Notion, and Slack. Uses hybrid search (vector + BM25) with re-ranking. Achieved 92% relevance score on internal benchmarks. Handles multi-tenant isolation via Pinecone namespaces.
Built with: Python, LangChain, Pinecone, FastAPI, React.

metadata.title: Real-time Fraud Monitor
metadata.text:
Project: Real-time Fraud Monitor (2020, production).
Event-driven fraud detection dashboard at BigBank Financial. Ingests transaction events from Kafka, applies ML scoring in real-time, and surfaces suspicious activity to analysts. Reduced average investigation time from 45 minutes to 25 minutes.
Built with: Python, Kafka, React, PostgreSQL, scikit-learn.

metadata.title: CollabBoard
metadata.text:
Project: CollabBoard (2022, archived).
Real-time collaborative whiteboard application built at StartupXYZ. Supports simultaneous editing by 50+ users with conflict-free replicated data types (CRDTs). WebSocket-based sync with offline support and automatic reconnection.
Built with: Next.js, TypeScript, WebSockets, Redis, Yjs.
Source code: https://github.com/aman/collabboard
